skill_clusters: Translating plain English rules into sequential logic
While and for loop control flow
State tracking across steps
Conditional branching per rule
Termination condition detection
Multi-step process simulation
constraints: All problems must be word problems written in plain prose. No LeetCode links. Each problem must describe a real-world process or protocol with explicit rules. Student must translate the rules into code without any pattern hints. Problems 1-7 easy, 8-14 medium, 15-20 hard. Difficulty increases by adding more rules, more edge cases, and more complex state to track. Reference problems: drone delivery protocol, warehouse sorting system, vending machine logic, traffic light sequencer.

skill_clusters: Format conversion between string representations
Splitting and joining on delimiters
Substring extraction and indexing
Edge case handling: leading zeros, mixed separators, empty strings
String to number conversion and back
Pattern matching without regex
Multi-field record parsing
constraints: All problems wrapped in real-world scenarios: log parsing, time format conversion, command parsing, record extraction. No abstract string problems. Problems 1-7 involve single format conversion. Problems 8-14 involve multi-step parsing pipelines. Problems 15-20 involve parsing with validation, error handling, and edge cases. Reference problems: bus departure time parser, CSV record extractor, command string interpreter, timestamp normalizer.

skill_clusters: Single array iteration with per-element computation
Parallel array traversal with zip
Running max, min, sum, average
Filtering elements by condition
Index tracking during traversal
Returning computed result with edge case handling
Ratio and rate computation across paired arrays
constraints: All problems framed as business scenarios: product ratings, sensor readings, price comparisons, score calculations. Problems 1-5 involve single array aggregation. Problems 6-10 involve two parallel arrays. Problems 11-15 involve filtering plus aggregation with edge cases like empty arrays, zero denominators, and ties. Reference problems: max rating-price ratio, highest scoring student, sensor anomaly detection, best value product finder.

skill_clusters: Building frequency maps from arrays
Lookup and membership testing in O(1)
Grouping elements by a property
Deduplication using sets and maps
Counting distinct elements
Finding most and least frequent elements
Two-array intersection and difference using hashmaps
constraints: All problems framed as real scenarios: inventory tracking, user activity logs, word frequency in documents, product category grouping. Problems 1-5 are pure frequency counting. Problems 6-10 involve grouping and lookup. Problems 11-15 involve combining two data sources using hashmaps with edge cases. No abstract problems. Every problem must be translatable from a word problem with no pattern label given.

skill_clusters: Sorting by single key
Sorting by multiple keys with tiebreakers
Custom comparator logic
Returning top N elements after sort
Rank assignment with dense and non-dense ranking
Stable sort behavior and when it matters
Sorting objects and records not just primitives
constraints: All problems wrapped in business scenarios: leaderboards, product rankings, employee performance sorting, race results. Problems 1-5 involve single-key sorting and returning top N. Problems 6-10 involve multi-key sorting with tiebreakers. Problems 11-15 involve rank assignment and handling ties with dense vs non-dense ranking. Every problem written as a word problem with no hints about sort direction or tiebreaker unless stated in the spec.

skill_clusters: Row and column swapping
Matrix rotation clockwise and counterclockwise
Row and column reversal
Sequential command parsing and execution on a grid
Transpose operation
Submatrix extraction
In-place modification vs new matrix creation
constraints: All problems framed as grid transformation scenarios: image processing, warehouse grid systems, spreadsheet operations, map rotation. Problems 1-5 involve single operations on a matrix. Problems 6-10 involve sequences of 3-5 operations applied in order. Problems 11-15 involve parsing a command string and applying operations dynamically with edge cases like non-square matrices and empty grids. Reference problem: matrix command parser.

skill_clusters: Converting time strings to total minutes or seconds
Computing differences between two timestamps
Filtering events by time window
Finding the most recent event before a given time
Handling 24-hour vs 12-hour formats
Day boundary and midnight wraparound
Sorting and ranking events by time
constraints: All problems framed as scheduling or logistics scenarios: bus departures, meeting overlaps, delivery windows, shift scheduling. Problems 1-5 involve simple time conversion and difference. Problems 6-10 involve filtering and finding relative events. Problems 11-15 involve multi-step time pipelines with edge cases like midnight boundaries, ties, and no valid result conditions. Reference problem: minutes since last bus departure.

skill_clusters: Fixed-size window max and min sum
Variable-size window expanding and contracting
Longest substring or subarray meeting a condition
Average over a rolling window
Window with frequency tracking inside
Identifying the window that optimizes a metric
Translating business data window problems into sliding window logic
constraints: All problems framed as data analysis scenarios: rolling sales averages, network packet windows, sensor reading windows, activity streak detection. Problems 1-4 are fixed-size windows. Problems 5-8 are variable-size windows. Problems 9-12 require identifying which approach to use from the word problem alone with no hints. Every problem written as a plain prose scenario with no mention of sliding window.

skill_clusters: Making locally optimal choices at each step
Advancing through a sequence with a pointer
Tracking cumulative cost or distance
Detecting when a greedy step terminates
Sorting as a preprocessing step before greedy
Multi-resource greedy allocation
Recognizing greedy problems from word problem framing
constraints: All problems framed as logistics or resource allocation scenarios: delivery routing, charging station networks, task scheduling, fuel stop optimization. Problems 1-4 involve straightforward single-pass greedy. Problems 5-8 involve greedy with preprocessing. Problems 9-12 are harder scenarios where the greedy choice is non-obvious from the word problem and requires careful reading. Reference problem: drone delivery foot distance calculator.

skill_clusters: Identifying the underlying pattern from a word problem alone
Mapping scenario keywords to data structures
Distinguishing simulation from sorting from hashmap problems
Stripping business language to find the computational core
Writing the pattern identification before writing any code
Handling deliberately misleading or verbose problem statements
Time-pressured pattern identification under OA conditions
constraints: These are mixed problems with no category labels. Each problem is a word problem drawn from any of the above topic areas. The exercise for each problem is two steps: first write which pattern this is and why in one sentence, then write the solution. Problems are scored on correct pattern identification as much as correct solution. Problems span all difficulty levels and all topic areas unpredictably. This set should only be attempted after the individual topic sets above feel automatic.
